1: wayyitḥazzaq Yᵉhôyādāʿ (‘Jehoiada took courage’) — the same verb ḥāzaq (H2388) used for Jehoram’s self-assertion (2 Chronicles 21:4) is here used positively: Jehoiada strengthened himself for righteous action. He enters ḇᵉrît (covenant) with five military commanders; the naming of all five (ʾAzaryāhû ben-Yᵉrôḥām, etc.) signals authentic historical memory. The covenant with commanders is the institutional mechanism for the Joash restoration.
2: wayyāsûḇû bᵉYᵉhûdāh wayyiqbᵉṣû ʾet-halᵉwiyyim (‘they traveled through Judah and assembled the Levites’) — the Levites as the military force for the temple-centered coup is distinctively Chronicler; 2 Kgs 11 uses the Carites (foreign mercenaries) instead. The Chronicler substitutes Levites throughout, consistent with his theological interest in proper cult personnel. rāʾšê hāʾāḇôt (‘heads of the ancestral families’) — bêt-hāʾāḇôt (H1): the kinship-group unit of clan organization in Chronicles.
3: wayyikrōt kol-haqqāhāl bᵉrît bᵉbêt hāʾĕlōhîm (‘all the assembly made a covenant in the house of God’) — the covenant takes place in the sanctuary, sanctifying the political act with divine witness. Jehoiada’s declaration: hinnēh ben-hammelek yimlōḵ kaʾăšer dibber YHWH ʿal-bᵉnê Dāwîd (‘the king’s son shall reign, as the LORD promised concerning the sons of David’) — the Davidic promise is the legal-theological ground for Joash’s right to the throne despite Athaliah’s occupation of it.
4: šōʿărîm bassippôt (‘gatekeepers at the temple entrances’) — šōʿēr (H7778): doorkeeper, gatekeeper; a distinct Levitical function (cf. 1 Chronicles 9:17-27). The three-way division of the guard force distributes oversight across the key access points: temple gates, palace, and the Foundation Gate. The Sabbath rotation is exploited to double the guard strength without breaking normal patterns of movement.
5: šaʿar hayyᵉsôd (‘Foundation Gate’) — the yᵉsôd (H3247): foundation, base; a gate name found only here. 2 Kings 11:6 reads ‘the gate of Sur’ instead — either different names for the same gate or different sources. The third station, ḥaṣrôt bêt-YHWH (‘courts of the LORD’s house’), is where the general populace stands as witnesses to the coronation.
6: wᵉhēm yibbōʾû kî-qᵉdōšîm hēm (‘for they are holy’) — the holiness of priests and Levites as grounds for exclusive sanctuary access is the foundational principle of Israelite sacred space. The common people (ʿam) keep the LORD’s watch but may not enter the inner areas. This principle (upheld even during a political coup) reflects the Chronicler’s consistent concern that cultic propriety not be violated even when urgent political action is required.
7: ʾîš wᵉkēlāyw bᵉyādô (‘each man with his weapons in hand’) — the armed Levites as temple guard; the Chronicler’s substitution of Levites for the Carites (2 Kings 11:4) makes the protective force ecclesiastically proper. wᵉkol-habbāʾ ʾel-habbayit yûmāt (‘anyone who tries to enter the house is to be put to death’) — the temple’s inviolability combined with the security cordon around the king.
8: kî lōʾ-šillēaḥ Yᵉhôyādāʿ hakkōhēn ʾet-hammaḥlᵉqôt (‘for Jehoiada did not release the divisions’) — maḥlāqôt (H4256): divisions, courses; the Levitical rotational system normally alternated weekly; Jehoiada kept both incoming and outgoing divisions active simultaneously, doubling the force without arousing suspicion about unusual mobilization.
9: hāʾźānôt wᵉhāšᵉlāṭîm wᵉhammāginnîm ʾăšer lammeleḵ Dāwîd (‘the spears, bucklers, and shields that had belonged to King David’) — the weapons stored as temple votive offerings since David’s reign (cf. 1 Samuel 21:9; 1 Chronicles 26:26-27). The issuing of David’s own weapons for Joash’s restoration ceremony adds symbolic weight: the Davidic king is restored using David’s own arsenal.
10: miyyāmîn habbayit wᵉʿad-śᵉmōʾl habbayit lammizᵉbēaḥ ûlabbayit (‘from the south side to the north side of the temple, along the altar and the house’) — the positioning of the armed guard forms a protective ring around the entire temple structure, with the king at center. The spatial description reflects careful military planning: no gap in the perimeter that would allow Athaliah’s supporters to intervene.
11: wayyittᵉnû ʿālāyw ʾet-hāʿēḏût (‘gave him the testimony’) — hāʿēḏût (H5715): the testimony, the covenant document; probably the Torah scroll (cf. Deuteronomy 17:18-20, where the king is to write a copy of the law and read it). The coronation of the Israelite king involved receiving the covenant document as symbol of his obligation to rule under YHWH’s law. The MKT renders ‘testimony’ as a Hebraism rather than ‘a copy of the covenant.’ wayyimšᵉḥûhû (‘anointed him’) — Jehoiada and his sons perform the royal unction; the priestly role in coronation is emphasized by the Chronicler.
12: wayyišmaʿ ʿAtalyāhû ʾet-qôl hāʿām hārāṣîm wᵉhammᵉhallᵉlîm ʾet-hammelek (‘Athaliah heard the noise of the people running and praising the king’) — the qôl (voice/noise) of praise is the alarm signal that betrays the coup to Athaliah. hārāṣîm (H7323): those running, the running crowd; mᵉhallᵉlîm (Piel participle of hālal, H1984): praising, singing praises — the worship shout that accompanies the coronation.
13: hinnēh hammelek ʿōmēd ʿal-hāʿammûd (‘the king standing by his pillar’) — hāʿammûd (H5982): the pillar; the king’s designated standing position at the temple entrance (cf. 2 Kings 23:3 for the same position used by Josiah). hašśārîm wᵉhaḥăṣōṣrôt ʿal-hammelek — the officers and trumpeters flanking the king ceremonially. ûmᵉšōrᵉrîm wᵉhammaddiᵉʿîm (‘singers and musicians leading praise’) — the full royal court ceremonial accompanies the coronation. wattiqqraʿ ʾAtalyāhû ʾet-bigdêhā — tearing garments as expression of horror: qešer qešer (‘treason, treason!’) — qeśer (H7195): conspiracy, treason.
14: wayyōṣēʾ Yᵉhôyādāʿ hakkōhēn ʾet-śārê hameʾôt pᵉqûdê hahayil (‘Jehoiada the priest brought out the captains who commanded the troops’) — pāqad (H6485): to appoint, command over. The command hôṣîʾû ʾōtāh miḇʿaḏ laśśᵉdērôt (‘take her outside the ranks’) — śᵉdērôt (H7713): rows, ranks; through/behind the guard ranks, away from the sanctuary area. The prohibition: wᵉhammāmît ʾaḥᵃrêhā haregû-ḥāreḇ (‘put to death anyone who follows her’) while keeping the execution outside the temple: kî ʾāmar hakkōhēn ʾal-tᵉmîtûhā bêt-YHWH (‘do not kill her in the LORD’s house’).
15: šaʿar sûs hammᵉbôʾ bêt-hammelek (‘the Horse Gate at the entrance to the palace’) — the Horse Gate was a palace entrance in Jerusalem (cf. Nehemiah 3:28; Jeremiah 31:40); a secular, administrative location chosen deliberately for Athaliah’s execution to keep blood-guilt away from the sanctuary precincts. The execution verb wayyᵉmîtûhā šām (‘they killed her there’) is unelaborated — the Chronicler is more interested in the principle (execution outside the temple) than the physical act.
16: wayyikrōt Yᵉhôyādāʿ bᵉrît bênô ûḇên kol-hāʿām ûḇên hammelek lihyôt lᵉʿam YHWH (‘Jehoiada made a covenant between himself, all the people, and the king, that they would be the LORD’s people’) — a three-party covenant: priest, people, and king, all bound to YHWH. The covenant formula lihyôt lᵉʿam YHWH (‘to be the LORD’s people’) is the covenantal commitment language of Sinai (Exodus 6:7; Jeremiah 30:22); Jehoiada’s covenant re-enacts the national covenant commitment as the basis for the restored monarchy.
17: wayyāḇōʾû kol-hāʿām bêt-habbāʿal wayyitṣûhû (‘all the people went to the house of Baal and tore it down’) — nāṯaṣ (H5422): to tear down, break down; iconoclasm as the immediate expression of covenant renewal. ûmattān kōhēn habbāʿal hāragû lipnê hammizbᵉḥôt (‘and killed Mattan the priest of Baal in front of the altars’) — the execution of the Baal priest at the altar of the destroyed shrine echoes Elijah’s killing of the Baal prophets at Carmel (1 Kings 18:40). The proper worship center (the temple) is restored by destroying the false one.
18: wayyāśem Yᵉhôyādāʿ pᵉqûddôt bêt-YHWH bᵉyad hakᵉhănîm halwîyyim (‘Jehoiada reestablished the temple administration under the priests and Levites’) — pᵉquddāh (H6486): oversight, appointed service. The phrase ʾăšer ḥālaq Dāwîd ʿal-bêt YHWH (‘the divisions David had appointed’) links the restored worship explicitly to the Davidic liturgical order, the Chronicler’s perpetual touchstone for correct worship. bᵉśimḥāh ûḇᵉšîr ʿal-yᵉdê Dāwîd (‘with the rejoicing and singing that David had ordained’) — Davidic music as liturgical standard.
19: wayyaʿᵃmēd šōʿᵃrîm ʿal-šaʿᵃrê bêt-YHWH (‘he stationed gatekeepers at the entrances to the LORD’s house’) — the restoration of proper gate-keeping reinstates the holiness barrier after Athaliah’s defilement. ûlᵉkol-ṭāmēʾ lᵉḵol-dāḇār (‘so that nothing unclean in any way could enter’) — ṭāmēʾ (H2931): ritually unclean; the holiness of the sanctuary requires that exclusion of impurity be systematically enforced, not assumed.
20: wayyōrîdû ʾet-hammelek mibêt YHWH (‘brought the king down from the LORD’s house’) — the procession moves from sanctuary (where coronation occurred) to throne room; the direction ‘down’ reflects Jerusalem’s topography (the temple mount is higher than the palace precincts). wayyôšîḇû ʾet-hammelek ʿal-kissēʾ hammamlāḵāh (‘seated the king on the royal throne’) — the political enthronement follows the religious coronation; the two steps together constitute the full installation of the Davidic king.
21: wayyiśmaḥ kol-ʿam-hāʾāreṣ wᵉhāʿîr šāqᵉṭāh (‘all the people of the land rejoiced, and the city was at peace’) — šāqaṭ (H8252): to be at rest, undisturbed, tranquil; used for post-war quiet after successful political transition. ʿam-hāʾāreṣ (‘people of the land’) is a sociological designation in Chronicles for the Judean citizenry as a political body with the power to validate or reject royal accessions. The closing formula wᵉʾAtalyāhû hêmîtû beḥāreḇ (‘Athaliah had been killed with the sword’) is syntactically subordinated to the peace that follows her removal.
